2. For one criterion, write the same lead question twice — once as a past-oriented behavioural question and once as a future-oriented situational question — and note in one line what each version would let you score that the other would not. *(composed)*
3. Take an advertised essential criterion verbatim and write a lead question whose wording a candidate could trace back to it without guessing. *(composed)*
4. Draft a four-to-six question set for a real vacancy and map each question to exactly one advertised essential criterion. Any question that maps to none, or to three, is redesigned. *(composed)*

5. Rewrite a lead question that currently contains two demands — for example "a time you led a team through change and delivered to deadline" — as two separate questions, or choose which single demand you are actually scoring. *(composed)*

6. Draft the same criterion's lead question at two role levels, and write the differing performance expectation for each, following the published pattern of one indicator producing different expected behaviour at two ranks. *(composed)*

text: 1. You have 30 seconds to prepare and two minutes to record. Describe a time you had to deliver work to a deadline you did not set. Say what you changed about how you worked, and what the outcome was. *(composed)*

text: 2. You have 30 seconds to prepare and 90 seconds to record. Tell us about a time you disagreed with a decision made by someone more senior than you. Cover what you said, how you said it, and what happened next. *(composed)*

text: 3. You have one minute to prepare and two minutes to record. Describe an occasion when you noticed a problem that was not your responsibility. What did you do, and what was the measurable effect? *(composed)*

level: Composed prompts written for timed asynchronous delivery
text: 4. In no more than two minutes: describe a time you had to explain something technical or unfamiliar to someone who needed to act on it. Tell us how you checked they had understood. *(composed)*

level: Composed prompts written for timed asynchronous delivery
text: 5. In no more than 90 seconds: tell us about a time you changed your approach because of feedback. Name the feedback, the change, and the difference it made. *(composed)*

text: 6. You have two minutes. Describe a situation where you had to work with limited information. Explain how you decided to proceed and how the decision turned out. *(composed)*

text: 7. You have two minutes. Tell us about a time you supported a colleague who was struggling while still meeting your own commitments. Include what you gave up to do it. *(composed)*

level: Composed prompts written for timed asynchronous delivery
text: 8. In no more than two minutes: describe a time your first attempt at something did not work. Tell us what you did second, and what you would do first if it happened again. *(composed)*

level: Composed prompts written for timed asynchronous delivery
text: 9. You have 90 seconds. Tell us about a time you had to prioritise between two things that both mattered. Explain your reasoning and the result. *(composed)*

text: 3. Record a version in which the Situation and Task together take no more than 20 seconds. Did the Action get more or less specific? *(composed)*

level: Adapting a STAR answer to a two-minute recording — drill prompts

1. You have described what the team did. What specifically was your individual contribution? *(composed — a probe reflecting the guidance to "Use 'I' not 'we'" and evidence your own role [4] [14])*

2. Why did you take that action rather than an alternative — what were you trying to achieve? *(composed — a values/reasoning probe in the style of the Situation–Task–Action–Result structure NICS recommends [14])*
3. What was the outcome, and what did you learn — especially if it did not go entirely to plan? *(composed — in the style of the "what did you learn" prompt in NICS interview guidance [14])*

4. Can you give a second, different example that shows the same competency in another setting? *(composed — reflecting the Welsh Government tip to prepare more examples than provided on the form [4])*
